summary: Cathy O'Neil critiques a CBS 'Eye on America' segment that gave uncritical airtime to Big Tech leaders like Yann LeCun to discuss AI fairness. She highlights the irony of LeCun now discussing AI fairness when he previously dismissed it as 'above his paygrade.' The piece examines how mainstream media amplifies industry talking points without skeptical voices, noting LeCun's contradictory positions on regulation and autonomous weapons. O'Neil argues this represents a collapse in AI journalism where powerful tech figures control the narrative about their own products' ethics.
key_points: CBS devoted an hour to letting Big Tech leaders self-report on AI fairness without critical counterpoints
LeCun previously dismissed AI fairness as 'above his paygrade' but now gets paid to discuss it on TV
Academia has been coopted into becoming propagandists for Big Tech rather than independent critics
LeCun opposes AI regulation while claiming to trust institutions to limit AI harms - a contradiction
Media should interview skeptics who can imagine alternative futures, not just industry insiders
money_quotes: Thinking about fairness in AI was 'above his paygrade,' a laughable comment even at the time, when he was the Head of Deep Learning at Facebook.
I'll bet there are few folks working there who actually get paid to think about fairness, and zero that get paid close to what LeCun gets paid.
LeCun maintains that fear of long term existential risk is pure sci-fi (even a broken clock!)
LeCun also 'sees an upside' in autonomous weapons, which he argues are 'necessary for the protection of democracy' instead of being good or bad.
If we have folks like LeCun weighing in on the ethics of AI and autonomous weapons, we must have reached a kind of event horizon in field of AI journalism, where nothing means anything.
How about interviewing someone else less optimistic and/or who can imagine another future?
key_insight: When media lets powerful tech executives be the primary voices on AI ethics and fairness, journalism has collapsed into corporate propaganda.
